audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Gideon's reduced army and Midianite defeat narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Jerubbaal/Gideon naming, fear-based troop release, water-lapping selection, dream and interpretation, jars/torches/trumpets, panic in Midianite camp, intertribal summons, Jordan fords, and Oreb/Zeeb execution.

verses.restored: Jerubbaal, that is Gideon, and all people with him rose early and camped by spring of Harod; camp of Midian was north of him, from hill of Moreh in valley.
YHWH said to Gideon, People with you are too many for me to give Midian into their hand, lest Israel boast over me, saying, My hand saved me.
Now call in ears of people, saying, Whoever is fearful and trembling, let him return and depart from Mount Gilead. Twenty-two thousand from people returned, and ten thousand remained.

YHWH said to Gideon, Still people are too many; bring them down to water, and I will refine them for you there. It shall be, of whom I say to you, This one shall go with you, he shall go; and of whom I say to you, This one shall not go with you, he shall not go.
He brought people down to water, and YHWH said to Gideon, Everyone who laps water with his tongue as dog laps, set him apart, and everyone who bows on knees to drink.
Number of those lapping with hand to mouth was three hundred men; all rest of people bowed on knees to drink water.
YHWH said to Gideon, By three hundred men who lapped I will save you and give Midian into your hand; let all people go, each man to his place.
They took provisions of people in their hand and their trumpets; every man of Israel he sent away to his tents, but three hundred men he strengthened/held. Camp of Midian was beneath him in valley.
It was that night YHWH said to him, Rise, go down against camp, for I have given it into your hand.
But if you fear to go down, go down, you and Purah your servant, to camp.
You shall hear what they speak, and afterward your hands will be strengthened to go down against camp. He went down with Purah his servant to edge of armed men in camp.
Midian, Amalek, and all sons of east were lying in valley like locusts for multitude, and their camels were without number, as sand on shore of sea for multitude.

Gideon came, and look, a man was recounting dream to his companion. He said, Look, I dreamed a dream, and look, a loaf of barley bread was tumbling into camp of Midian; it came to tent, struck it, and it fell, turned it upward, and tent fell.
His companion answered and said, This is nothing except sword of Gideon son of Joash, man of Israel. Divine has given Midian and all camp into his hand.
It was, when Gideon heard recounting of dream and its interpretation, that he bowed down. He returned to camp of Israel and said, Rise, for YHWH has given camp of Midian into your hand.
He divided three hundred men into three companies, and gave trumpets in hand of all of them, and empty jars, with torches inside jars.
He said to them, Look at me and do likewise; and look, when I come to edge of camp, as I do, so you shall do.
When I blow trumpet, I and all who are with me, then you shall blow trumpets also around all camp and say, For YHWH and for Gideon!
Gideon came, and hundred men with him, to edge of camp at beginning of middle watch, when they had just set watch. They blew trumpets and shattered jars that were in their hands.
Three companies blew trumpets and broke jars; they held torches in left hands and trumpets in right hands to blow, and cried, Sword for YHWH and for Gideon!
They stood each man in his place around camp, and all camp ran, shouted, and fled.
Three hundred blew trumpets, and YHWH set sword of each man against his companion and against all camp; camp fled as far as Beth-shittah toward Zererah, as far as edge of Abel-meholah by Tabbath.
Men of Israel were called out from Naphtali, Asher, and all Manasseh, and they pursued after Midian.

Gideon sent messengers through all hill country of Ephraim, saying, Come down against Midian and capture waters before them as far as Beth-barah and Jordan. Every man of Ephraim was called out, and they captured waters as far as Beth-barah and Jordan.

They captured two princes of Midian, Oreb and Zeeb; they killed Oreb at rock of Oreb, and Zeeb they killed at winepress of Zeeb. They pursued Midian and brought heads of Oreb and Zeeb to Gideon across Jordan.

translation_notes: The army reduction is framed as preventing Israel from claiming self-salvation; it is not a model for reckless under-resourcing or forcing vulnerable people into danger.
Those who are fearful are explicitly permitted to return. Fear is not shamed or punished.
The water-lapping test is opaque and has generated many interpretations; the text itself does not explain the selection morally or psychologically.
Gideon is still afraid after the fleece signs, and YHWH accommodates his fear through Purah and the overheard dream. This continues the chapter 6 pattern of reassurance rather than ridicule.
The barley loaf dream uses humble food imagery to symbolize the collapse of Midian's tent-power.
The night attack relies on psychological panic with trumpets, shattered jars, and torches; the enemy camp turns swords against itself.
The chapter ends with pursuit and decapitated enemy leaders. The victory may be narrated as deliverance, but the trophy-head scene remains violent and non-normative.
safety_note: Do not use Judges 7 to shame fear, romanticize tiny armies, manipulate people with dreams, glorify psychological warfare, or celebrate trophy violence. Center reassurance, humility against self-exaltation, and the refusal to make fear incompatible with calling.
